# FlowScript — Complete Language Specification for LLMs

Version: current (see package.json for semver)
Canonical examples: test/fixtures/*.flow
Public API: src/index.ts

---

## Overview

FlowScript is a plain-text DSL that compiles to SVG flowcharts.
Pipeline: parse → layout → route → renderSVG

One-shot usage:
```typescript
import { render } from 'flowscript';
const svg = render(`
  #start Begin
    Process Data
    #end Done
`);
```

Stepwise:
```typescript
import { parse, layout, route, renderSVG, cleanTheme } from 'flowscript';
const doc = parse(source);
layout(doc);
const routes = route(doc);
const svg = renderSVG(doc, routes, { theme: cleanTheme });
```

---

## File structure

A .flow file has three optional sections in this order:

1. Frontmatter block (YAML-style, between `---` lines)
2. Directives (lines starting with `@`)
3. Node definitions (everything else)

```
---
title: My Diagram
author: Alice
---

@theme clean
@direction TB

#start Entry Point
  Step One
  #end Done
```

---

## Frontmatter

Optional. Delimited by `---` lines. Supports arbitrary key: value pairs.
Only `title` and `subtitle` are rendered in SVG output; others are metadata.

```
---
title: Incident Response
subtitle: Security Operations Center Protocol
author: Guy Schamp
version: 2.1
---
```

---

## Directives

Directives configure the layout and renderer. All optional.

```
@theme clean          # visual theme (clean is the only built-in default)
@direction TB         # flow direction: TB (default), BT, LR, RL
@routing orthogonal   # edge routing: orthogonal (default), bezier, polyline
@line-jumps on        # Visio-style arc hops at crossings: on (default), off
@corner-radius 8      # orthogonal edge corner radius in px
@spacing 60           # dagre node spacing
@layout grid          # layout engine: grid (default for TB), dagre
```

---

## Nodes

### Plain text → process rectangle (most common shape)

```
Step One
Validate Input
Send Confirmation Email
```

Each plain-text line at the current indent level becomes a rectangular process node.
Nodes at the same indent level under a parent are chained sequentially (implicit edges).

### Keyword shapes

All keyword shapes use the pattern: `#keyword Label text`

| Keyword       | SVG shape           | Use for                        |
|---------------|---------------------|--------------------------------|
| `#start`      | Rounded rectangle   | Entry point (one per flow)     |
| `#end`        | Rounded rectangle   | Terminal (multiple allowed)    |
| `#decision`   | Diamond             | Branching condition            |
| `#subprocess` | Double-border rect  | Reusable sub-process           |
| `#io`         | Parallelogram       | Input / output                 |
| `#data`       | Cylinder            | Database or data store         |
| `#circle`     | Circle              | Connector / junction           |
| `#note`       | Folded-corner rect  | Annotation                     |
| `#manual`     | Trapezoid           | Manual operation               |
| `#delay`      | Half-rounded rect   | Wait or timer                  |

```
#start User Visits Page
  Validate Session
  #subprocess Load User Profile
  #io Display Dashboard
  #decision Session Expired?
    -> yes: #end Logout
    -> no: Continue
  #data Save Activity Log
  #end Done
```

---

## Connections (edges)

### Implicit chaining

Nodes at the same indentation level under a shared parent connect top-to-bottom automatically.
This is the primary way to build linear sequences.

```
#start Begin
  Step One
  Step Two
  Step Three
  #end Done
```

Result: Begin → Step One → Step Two → Step Three → Done

### Explicit arrow `->`

Use `->` to connect any two named nodes, bypassing indentation hierarchy.
The source and target are matched by their exact label text.

```
Step One -> Step Two
Step One -> Step Two: "with a label"
```

Cross-section connections (required for groups, swimlanes, and non-sequential flows):
```
Submit -> Review
Review -> Approve
Approve -> #end Complete
```

### Retry / dashed edge `~>`

`~>` works identically to `->` but renders the path as a dashed line.
Use it for loop-backs, retries, or any "soft" / cyclical connection.

```
#decision Retry?
  -> yes: #end Done
  ~> no: Validate Input
```

For backward compatibility, edges with label text exactly `"try again"` or `"resend"`
also render dashed when written with `->`. Prefer `~>` in new diagrams.

---

## Decisions

### Basic two-way decision

```
#decision Approved?
  -> yes: Next Step
  -> no: Rejected
```

### Single-quote condition syntax (preferred — no ambiguity with colons in labels)

```
#decision Approved?
  -> 'Yes' Next Step
  -> 'No'  Rejected
```

Conditions are matched case-insensitively.
`yes` / `Yes` / `YES` are all equivalent.
`no` / `No` / `NO` are all equivalent.

### Multi-way branching

```
#decision Priority?
  -> 'P1' Page On-Call
  -> 'P2' Assign Team Lead
  -> 'P3' Add to Backlog
```

### Decision with loop-back (cycle / retry)

Reference an existing node's exact label to create a back-edge.

```
#decision Ready?
  -> yes: #end Complete
  -> no: Prepare
```

Here `Prepare` must already exist as a node earlier in the file (or be defined elsewhere).
The router creates a back-edge automatically.

### Inline #end / #start on a branch

```
#decision Valid?
  -> yes: #end Success
  -> no: #end Failure
```

This creates two distinct end nodes inline. Each gets its own shape.

---

## Loop-backs

Any `->` or branch that names an existing node label creates a loop-back (back-edge).
There is no special syntax — just reuse the exact label.

```
Validate Input
#decision Valid?
  -> yes: Save Record
  -> no: Validate Input     ← back-edge: references node defined above
```

For retry/dashed rendering on the loop:
```
#decision Valid?
  -> yes: Save Record
  ~> no: Validate Input
```

---

## Groups

`#group` defines a labeled container. Nodes indented under it are its children.
Children chain implicitly and are visually enclosed in the group box.

```
#group Payment Processing
  Validate Card
  Charge Card
  Send Receipt

#group Fulfillment
  Pick Items
  Pack Order
  Ship Order

// Cross-group connections must be explicit
Send Receipt -> Pick Items
```

### CRITICAL: #group cannot be used inline as a reference or jump target

WRONG — this creates an empty duplicate group, not a connection:
```
Step One
  #group My Group    ← INVALID: not a jump/pointer
```

CORRECT — end the current section, then define the group block:
```
Step One
Step Two

#group My Group
  First Child
  Second Child
```

To connect into a group, use an explicit edge to the group's first child node:
```
Step Two -> First Child
```

---

## Swimlanes

`#lane` partitions nodes into labeled horizontal (or vertical) swim-lane columns.
Nodes inside a lane are implicitly chained. Cross-lane connections must be explicit.

```
#lane Customer
  #start Report Issue
  Provide Details
  #end Resolved

#lane Support Agent
  Triage Ticket
  #decision Severity?
    -> high: Escalate to Engineering
    -> low: Apply Known Fix

#lane Engineering
  Investigate Root Cause
  Deploy Fix

// Cross-lane edges (always explicit)
Provide Details -> Triage Ticket
Escalate to Engineering -> Investigate Root Cause
Deploy Fix -> Resolved
Apply Known Fix -> Resolved
```

Swimlanes automatically use the dagre layout engine (not grid).
`@direction TB` is supported; `@direction LR` is also valid for row-based lanes.

---

## Comments

Full-line comments only. Inline comments (end of a node line) are not supported.

```
// This is a valid comment
#start Begin
  Step One    // INVALID: this text is part of the node label, not a comment
  Step Two
// Another comment
  #end Done
```

---

## What FlowScript does NOT support

These are common mistakes LLMs make. None of the following are valid syntax:

❌ Parenthetical annotations:
```
#start Active Shooter Alert Received
  (Cameras / Alarms / Reports / Calls)    ← INVALID: becomes a process node
```

❌ Bullet / sub-item lists:
```
Share Intel Package
  - Last known shooter location           ← INVALID: becomes a process node labeled "- Last known shooter location"
  - Number of shooters                    ← INVALID: same
```

❌ Inline `#group` references (pointer / jump syntax):
```
Step One
  #group My Group     ← INVALID: creates empty group, not a connection
```

❌ Dead-end "Continue X" placeholder nodes (nodes with no outgoing edges that are loop targets):
```
#decision Resolved?
  -> yes: Done
  -> no: Continue Monitoring    ← if "Continue Monitoring" has no edges, it's a dead end
```
Fix: point directly at the loop target, or add an explicit back-edge from "Continue Monitoring".

❌ Annotations / notes attached to edges (no edge metadata syntax):
```
Step One -> Step Two (note: check logs first)    ← INVALID
```
Use a `#note` node or a labeled edge instead:
```
Step One -> Step Two: "check logs first"
```

❌ Nested decision indentation inside an unrelated section:
```
Immediate Actions
  Do Thing
    #decision Confirmed?    ← indented under "Do Thing" — this creates a decision
                               as a child of "Do Thing", not of "Immediate Actions"
```
Decisions attach to their immediate parent in the indentation hierarchy.
Keep decision blocks one level under the section they belong to.

---

## Indentation rules

- Use consistent indentation (2 or 4 spaces, or tabs — pick one per file)
- Indent level determines parent-child (sequential chain) relationship
- Children of a node chain linearly: A → B → C → ...
- A `#decision` block and its `->` branches must be indented under their logical parent
- After a decision, continuing siblings at the same level chain from the decision's last output

```
#start Begin
  Validate          ← child of Begin
  #decision OK?     ← child of Begin, chained after Validate
    -> yes: Save    ← branch (not a child of the decision, but its target)
    -> no: Error    ← branch
  Notify            ← back at Begin's child level — chained after decision
  #end Done
```

---

## Worked examples

### Example 1: Linear flow

```
#start User Visits Signup Page
  Enter Email & Password
  Send Verification Email
  #end Registration Complete
```

### Example 2: Decision with loop

```
#start Submit Form
  Validate Input
  #decision Valid?
    -> yes: Save Record
    ~> no: Validate Input
  Save Record
  #end Done
```

### Example 3: Multi-level decisions with retry

```
---
title: Learning Flow
---

#start Begin Lesson
  Read Introduction
  #subprocess Watch Demo Video
  #decision Did you understand the demo?
    -> yes: Take Practice Quiz
    -> no: Re-watch Demo Video
  Re-watch Demo Video ~> Watch Demo Video: "try again"
  Take Practice Quiz
  #decision Quiz score 80%+?
    -> yes: Advance to Project
    -> no: Review Concepts
  Review Concepts
  #decision Ready to retake quiz?
    -> yes: Take Practice Quiz
    -> no: Schedule Tutoring
  Schedule Tutoring -> Attend Tutoring Session
  Attend Tutoring Session -> Take Practice Quiz
  Advance to Project
    #subprocess Build Capstone Project
    #decision Project meets criteria?
      -> yes: Submit For Review
      -> no: Iterate On Project
    Iterate On Project ~> Build Capstone Project: "iterate"
    Submit For Review
    #decision Reviewer approved?
      -> yes: #end Lesson Complete
      -> no: Iterate On Project
```

### Example 4: Security operations procedure (correct version of a Grok-generated diagram)

```
---
title: GSOC Active Shooter Response
subtitle: Security Operations Center Protocol
---

#start Active Shooter Alert Received

Verify & Confirm Incident

#decision Confirmed?
  -> yes: Immediate Actions
  -> no: Verify & Confirm Incident

Immediate Actions
  Activate Site Lockdown
  Trigger Mass Notification
  Dispatch On-Site Security Teams
  Call 911

#group Monitoring & Coordination
  Track Shooter via CCTV
  Coordinate Field Security Teams
  Relay Updates to Law Enforcement
  Manage Internal Communications

  #decision Shooter Neutralized or Contained?
    -> yes: LE Arrival & Handover
    -> no: Track Shooter via CCTV

Immediate Actions -> Track Shooter via CCTV

LE Arrival & Handover
  Share Full Intel Package
  Provide Physical Access Support
  Assist with Perimeter Security

  #decision Incident Stabilized?
    -> yes: Recovery Phase
    -> no: LE Arrival & Handover

Recovery Phase
  Personnel Accountability
  Support LE Investigation
  Activate Business Continuity Plan
  Debrief & After-Action Review

#end Incident Resolved
```

---

## Quick reference: condition keywords

The following condition strings receive special routing treatment
(yes-branch exits diamond bottom; no-branch exits diamond side):

- Positive: `yes`, `true` (case-insensitive)
- Negative: `no`, `false` (case-insensitive)

All other condition strings (e.g. `high`, `P1`, `approved`) are treated
as neutral — the router uses geometry to pick exit direction.

---

## SVG output conventions

Every rendered SVG has:
- `class="fs-diagram"` on the root `<svg>`
- `<g class="fs-edges">` containing all edge groups
- `<g class="fs-nodes">` containing all node groups
- Each node: `<g class="fs-node" data-node-id="nN" data-shape="TYPE">`
- Each edge: `<g class="fs-edge" data-from="nN" data-to="nN">`
- Edge paths: `<path class="fs-edge-path">` (retry edges add `fs-edge-retry`)
- Edge labels: `<text class="fs-edge-label">`

Node IDs are assigned sequentially in parse order: `n1`, `n2`, `n3`, …

JavaScript selectors:
```javascript
svg.querySelector('[data-node-id="n3"]')        // node by ID
svg.querySelectorAll('[data-shape="decision"]')  // all diamonds
svg.querySelectorAll('[data-from="n3"]')         // edges leaving n3
svg.querySelectorAll('[data-to="n3"]')           // edges entering n3
```

---

## Source files for deeper reference

| Path | Contents |
|------|----------|
| `src/parser/ast.ts` | AST types: FlowDocument, FlowNode, FlowEdge, FlowGroup, FlowLane |
| `src/parser/lexer.ts` | Token types and tokenization rules |
| `src/parser/parser.ts` | Recursive descent parser |
| `src/layout/grid-layout.ts` | Grid layout engine (TB default) |
| `src/layout/router.ts` | Orthogonal edge router |
| `src/render/svg.ts` | SVG renderer |
| `src/themes/clean.ts` | Default theme tokens |
| `test/fixtures/` | Canonical .flow examples (ground truth) |
